name: Excessive Agency
what_it_means: LLM agents are granted too many permissions, access to too many tools, or too much autonomy — enabling attackers to abuse the agent to perform destructive or unauthorized actions on connected systems.
animated_example: Like giving an intern a master key to every room in the building and letting them act on verbal instructions from anyone — including strangers.
quick_checks: Are LLM agents restricted to the minimum set of tools and permissions needed for their task?
Are irreversible or high-impact actions (deletes, sends, payments) gated behind human approval?
Is the scope of each tool call logged and auditable?
Can injected instructions from external content trigger high-privilege tool calls?

payload_types.description: LLM with broad tool access performs destructive actions not intended by the user
examples: Agent with file deletion tool removes critical system files
Agent with email access sends phishing emails
Agent with admin API access modifies production configuration
mitigation: Apply least-privilege — grant only the minimum tools required for each workflow
Separate read and write permissions for every tool integration

payload_types.description: Injected content causes an agent to call tools on behalf of an attacker
examples: Email body instructs mail-reading agent to forward all messages to attacker
Web page instructs browsing agent to exfiltrate session cookies
Document instructs code agent to run malicious shell commands
mitigation: Require explicit human confirmation before any action triggered by external content
Sandbox tool calls initiated by indirect inputs

payload_types.description: LLM agent executes long chains of actions without checkpoints or user oversight
examples: Agent autonomously deploys code to production after interpreting a Slack message
Agent executes a multi-step purchasing workflow based on email instruction
Agent modifies database records autonomously in response to user query
mitigation: Break long agentic workflows into approval-gated steps
Implement circuit breakers that pause execution for human review

payload_types.description: Agent combines tools in ways that exceed their individual intended scope
examples: Web search + code execution combined to exfiltrate data via DNS
File read + email send combined to exfiltrate confidential documents
Calendar read + external API combined to expose scheduling data
mitigation: Monitor and alert on unusual tool combination patterns
Define allowed tool chains explicitly; deny unlisted combinations

how_to_test: Attempt to trigger destructive tool calls (delete, send, modify) via injected instructions in external content
Verify the agent cannot send emails, post data, or execute code without explicit user confirmation
Test tool scope: can the agent access systems outside its defined operational domain?
Check if long autonomous action chains can be triggered from a single user message or external document
Review audit logs to verify every tool call is attributed and traceable to an authorized request
expected_outcomes: Agent cannot perform destructive or irreversible actions without explicit human approval
Injected instructions from external content cannot trigger tool calls
All agent actions are logged with full attribution and are auditable
minimal_example: Email instructs agent to delete all calendar events → agent prompts user: 'Confirm deletion of 47 events?' → user declines → no action taken
mitigation: Apply strict least-privilege to all LLM agent integrations — grant only the minimum set of tools, APIs, and permissions required for the specific workflow, and remove unused capabilities entirely

Gate all irreversible, destructive, or high-impact actions (file deletion, emails, payments, deployments) behind explicit human confirmation steps — never allow autonomous execution of such actions based solely on LLM output

Implement comprehensive audit logging for every agent tool call: log who initiated the session, which tool was called, with what parameters, and what result was returned — store logs in a tamper-resistant system
Enforce action scope boundaries so that tool calls initiated by external/indirect inputs (emails, documents, web pages) are automatically sandboxed and require explicit user approval before execution
